stem:
A 24-day-old term infant is brought to the emergency department with a temperature recorded at home of 38.4 degrees and one day of poor feeding. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, exclusively breastfed, and gained weight at the maternal child health nurse review three days ago. There has been no rash, no vomiting, and the mother had no fevers or rash in pregnancy. The maternal Group B Streptococcus swab at 36 weeks was negative.

On examination he is alert but reduced in spontaneous movement. The anterior fontanelle is soft. There is no rash, no bulging fontanelle, no clinical jaundice, and capillary return is two seconds. The chest is clear, the abdomen is soft without organomegaly, and the umbilical stump is dry. Vital signs are within normal limits except temperature 38.2 degrees.

data_table.Investigations: venous blood gas pH 7.36 lactate 2.0 mmol/L, capillary glucose 3.6 mmol/L, urinalysis nitrites negative leucocytes negative, paired blood cultures and urine sent, chest radiograph clear, viral respiratory panel pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Observe in short stay, repeat temperature in two hours, and discharge with general practice review if cultures negative
options.rationale: Infants under three months with fever cannot be discharged based on a single short observation. Bacterial meningitis and bacteraemia are missed by relying on clinical appearance alone in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant)

options.text: Admit, perform lumbar puncture, take blood and urine cultures, and start intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin covers Group B Streptococcus and Listeria but provides inadequate cover for Gram negative meningitis in a four week old. RCH and Therapeutic Guidelines specify cefotaxime (with amoxicillin/ampicillin for Listeria cover if under one month) as the empirical choice. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant)

options.text: Admit, take blood and urine cultures, start intravenous flucloxacillin and gentamicin, and defer the lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Deferring lumbar puncture in a febrile infant under three months risks missing meningitis (cell counts and protein normalise within hours of antibiotic exposure). Flucloxacillin/gentamicin misses pneumococcal and listerial cover. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant)

options.text: Admit, perform lumbar puncture, take blood and urine cultures, and start intravenous cefotaxime plus benzylpenicillin
options.rationale: Infants under four weeks with fever require a full septic workup including lumbar puncture and empirical intravenous cefotaxime plus amoxicillin or benzylpenicillin to cover Listeria monocytogenes (which is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins). After 4 weeks, cefotaxime monotherapy is generally adequate. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant)

options.text: Send respiratory virus polymerase chain reaction panel and discharge if positive for any seasonal respiratory virus
options.rationale: A positive viral PCR does not exclude concurrent serious bacterial infection in this age group; up to 7 percent of febrile infants under three months with a positive viral PCR also have a serious bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Infant)

explanation.summary: Any infant under three months with a documented fever of 38.0 degrees or above requires a full septic workup (full blood count, blood culture, urine culture, lumbar puncture, chest radiograph if respiratory features) and empirical intravenous antibiotics in Australia. The pathogen profile is age dependent: Group B Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, and Listeria dominate under one month; pneumococcus and meningococcus rise after one month. Cefotaxime plus amoxicillin (Listeria cover) is the empirical regimen for under-four-weeks; cefotaxime alone after that.
key_points: Fever 38.0 or above under three months mandates full septic workup including LP
Under 4 weeks: cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 6 to 8 hours plus amoxicillin or benzylpenicillin for Listeria
4 weeks to 3 months: cefotaxime alone usually adequate; reassess for Listeria risk
Positive viral PCR does NOT exclude concurrent serious bacterial infection
Co-amoxiclav alone is inadequate; cephalosporins do not cover Listeria
explanation.pearls: Listeria monocytogenes is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins - cover Listeria with amoxicillin or benzylpenicillin in any febrile infant under four weeks, even when cefotaxime is already running.

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is referred from a regional emergency department on day 12 of a quotidian fever spiking to 40 degrees once daily, with a salmon-coloured rash that comes and goes with the temperature. He has had two doses of IVIG and high-dose aspirin at his local hospital under a presumptive diagnosis of incomplete Kawasaki disease, but his fevers have not resolved.

On arrival to the tertiary centre he is irritable, pale, with hepatosplenomegaly and a non-tender right knee effusion. A baseline echocardiogram from day 7 was reported as normal coronary arteries. There is no conjunctival injection, no oral changes, no peripheral oedema, and no cervical lymphadenopathy now. The salmon rash appears during a temperature spike during the assessment and fades as the fever settles two hours later.

data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 88 g/L (110-145), platelets 142 x10^9/L falling from 612 a week ago, white cells 24.6 x10^9/L (5-15) neutrophils 19.8, ferritin 18400 mcg/L (15-150), triglycerides 4.8 mmol/L (<2.3), fibrinogen 1.4 g/L (1.5-4), AST 412 U/L, sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), CRP 96 mg/L, ESR 12 mm/hr disproportionately low for CRP, D-dimer 8.6 mg/L

lead_in: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Immunoglobulin resistant complete Kawasaki disease needing second dose immunoglobulin and infliximab
options.rationale: Persistent quotidian fever with evanescent rash and falling ESR despite rising ferritin is not the Kawasaki pattern, and the absence of mucocutaneous features now (with normal coronaries on day 7) argues against KD. IVIG resistance in true KD would still show coronary inflammation or partial mucocutaneous response. (Source: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA)
source_refs: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA

options.text: Systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis complicated by macrophage activation syndrome
options.rationale: Quotidian fever with evanescent salmon rash, hepatosplenomegaly, an inflammatory knee effusion, IVIG resistance, and the laboratory signature of MAS (extreme hyperferritinaemia, falling platelets and ESR, rising triglycerides, falling fibrinogen, transaminitis, hyponatraemia) define sJIA-MAS. The 'paradoxical fall' in ESR with persistently high ferritin is highly suggestive. (Source: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA)
source_refs: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA

options.text: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia with diffuse bone marrow infiltration and cytokine release
options.rationale: ALL typically produces pancytopenia with circulating blasts, marrow failure, and bone pain rather than the inflammatory cytokine storm shown here. The neutrophilia of 19.8, very high ferritin, and triglyceride rise fit MAS, not leukaemia. (Source: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA)
source_refs: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA

options.text: Visceral leishmaniasis complicated by secondary haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis
options.rationale: Visceral leishmaniasis is rare in non-endemic Australian children and the absence of travel history makes it improbable. Secondary HLH from infection would still need Leishmania exposure that is not described. (Source: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA)
source_refs: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA

options.text: Periodic fever, aphthous stomatitis, pharyngitis, and adenitis syndrome decompensation
options.rationale: PFAPA presents with recurrent stereotyped fever episodes (every 3 to 6 weeks lasting 3 to 5 days) with pharyngitis, aphthous ulcers, and adenitis - a fundamentally different temporal pattern with normal laboratories between episodes. (Source: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA)
source_refs: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA

explanation.summary: Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS, secondary HLH) complicates around 10 percent of systemic JIA. The diagnostic signature is extreme hyperferritinaemia (often greater than 10000 mcg/L), a paradoxical fall in ESR despite high CRP, falling platelets and white cells, rising triglycerides, falling fibrinogen, transaminitis, and hyponatraemia. Untreated mortality approaches 20 percent. First line treatment is pulse methylprednisolone with anakinra (IL-1 blocker) as steroid-sparing; cyclosporine is added for refractory disease. Recognition requires actively considering MAS in any sJIA child with worsening fever, falling counts, or rising ferritin.
key_points: Quotidian (once daily) fever with evanescent salmon rash is sJIA
MAS signature: ferritin > 10000, falling ESR with rising CRP, low fibrinogen, high triglycerides, transaminitis, hyponatraemia
First line MAS: IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg/day pulses with anakinra 2 to 4 mg/kg/day
Differentiated from KD by quotidian fever, evanescent rash, lack of coronary involvement, IVIG resistance
PRINTO 2016 MAS classification criteria operationalise the laboratory pattern
explanation.pearls: Ferritin above 10000 mcg/L in a child with persistent inflammation has a positive likelihood ratio above 50 for MAS - escalate to pulse methylprednisolone and anakinra without waiting for bone marrow haemophagocytosis.

sources.label: Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health: Systemic JIA
RCH CPG: Fever of Unknown Origin

stem:
A 32-year-old primigravida is admitted to the birth suite at 34 plus 2 weeks gestation with a 24 hour history of a frontal headache and 'spots' in her vision. She has been monitored as an outpatient for proteinuria (1+) since 30 weeks. Her partner says she was confused for a few minutes at home but recovered before they arrived.

On examination she is alert and oriented now. Knee jerks are 3+ and symmetrical. The uterus is non-tender, the cervix is closed on speculum, and the cardiotocograph baseline is 140 with two accelerations and no decelerations over a 30 minute trace. Her partner reports she had a single self-limiting tonic-clonic seizure on the corridor as they walked into the assessment area, lasting 90 seconds. Maternal vital signs and bloods are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 102, blood pressure 172/112, respiratory rate 18, SpO2 98 percent on room air, temperature 36.7 degrees, foetal heart rate 140
data_table.Investigations: platelets 88 x10^9/L (150-400), ALT 142 U/L (<40), creatinine 96 micromol/L (45-90), urine protein:creatinine 320 mg/mmol (<30), lactate dehydrogenase 612 U/L, haptoglobin within normal limits, urate 0.42 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Immediate caesarean section without antiseizure prophylaxis on the basis that delivery is the definitive cure for eclampsia
options.rationale: Delivery is definitive but maternal stabilisation with MgSO4 and antihypertensive control precedes operative birth to reduce maternal stroke and recurrent seizure risk. Operating on an unstable patient increases morbidity. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 10 mg bolus and intravenous phenytoin loading at 18 mg/kg, with antihypertensive therapy afterward
options.rationale: Diazepam and phenytoin are inferior to MgSO4 in eclampsia (Collaborative Eclampsia Trial showed 50 percent fewer recurrent seizures with MgSO4 than phenytoin and 67 percent fewer than diazepam). Australian guidelines specify MgSO4 first line. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g intravenously over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour, with labetalol to target blood pressure under 160/110
options.rationale: After an eclamptic seizure (single tonic-clonic seizure with documented severe pre-eclampsia), MgSO4 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours is the Australian standard alongside antihypertensive control targeting under 160/110 (labetalol 20 to 40 mg IV bolus, repeatable). MgSO4 reduces recurrent eclampsia by 50 percent versus placebo. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine 5 mg bolus every 20 minutes targeting blood pressure under 130/80 without antiseizure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Target blood pressure in severe pre-eclampsia is under 160/110 (not under 130/80), and antiseizure prophylaxis with MgSO4 is mandatory after an eclamptic seizure. Over-rapid blood pressure lowering risks placental hypoperfusion. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg and admit for outpatient blood pressure and proteinuria surveillance
options.rationale: Although antenatal corticosteroids are given at 34+2 if delivery is anticipated within seven days, they do not address the immediate need for MgSO4, blood pressure control, and planning for delivery. Outpatient surveillance is unsafe after an eclamptic seizure. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Eclampsia is defined as a generalised seizure on a background of pre-eclampsia. Immediate priorities are maternal airway and stabilisation, MgSO4 loading 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours, blood pressure control to less than 160/110 with labetalol or hydralazine, and planning urgent delivery once stable. MgSO4 also reduces severe maternal morbidity (placental abruption, ICU admission) by around 25 percent in severe pre-eclampsia without seizures.
key_points: MgSO4 first line for eclampsia (loading 4 g IV over 20 min, then 1 g/hour for 24 hours)
Target BP < 160/110 with labetalol 20 to 40 mg IV bolus repeatable, or hydralazine 5 mg IV bolus
Monitor Mg toxicity: respiratory rate, deep tendon reflexes, urine output - reverse with calcium gluconate 1 g IV
MgSO4 also indicated in severe pre-eclampsia without seizure to prevent eclampsia
Delivery is definitive but maternal stabilisation precedes operative birth
explanation.pearls: MgSO4 toxicity manifests at serum magnesium 3.5 mmol/L (loss of patellar reflex), 5 mmol/L (respiratory depression), and 6 mmol/L (cardiac arrest) - reverse with 10 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate IV over 10 minutes.

stem:
A 33-year-old G3P2 woman has just delivered a 3.9 kg baby by spontaneous vaginal birth at 40 plus 3 weeks after a four hour active second stage. She received active third stage management with 10 units intramuscular oxytocin. The placenta delivered complete at 12 minutes. Twenty minutes after delivery she has soaked through two large pads with fresh red blood and a third is in progress. The midwife has called for help and started bimanual uterine massage.

On examination the uterus is large and soft despite massage. There is no perineal or vaginal wall tear on inspection, the cervix appears intact, and the placenta is confirmed complete. Two 16 gauge cannulas are in. Her booking blood pressure was 132/86 and antenatal blood pressure has remained around 135/85 throughout pregnancy. She has no asthma. Vital signs are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 116, blood pressure 102/64, respiratory rate 20, SpO2 99 percent on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees
data_table.Investigations: estimated blood loss 1100 mL and ongoing, haemoglobin pre-delivery 124 g/L, group and screen on file, fibrinogen pending

lead_in: After ongoing uterine massage and an infusion of oxytocin 40 units in 1 L normal saline, which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Intramuscular carboprost (15-methyl PGF2 alpha) 250 micrograms with intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Carboprost is the third line uterotonic after oxytocin and ergometrine. Skipping ergometrine misses a safe, cheap, first-after-oxytocin step in a woman with no contraindication to ergot agents. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Intramuscular ergometrine 250 micrograms with concurrent intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: After oxytocin infusion, the next uterotonic in the Australian PPH algorithm is ergometrine 250 micrograms IM or 250 micrograms slow IV (avoid in hypertension, severe cardiac disease, or pre-eclampsia). Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within three hours of birth (WOMAN trial) reduces death from bleeding by around one third and is given concurrently. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Sublingual misoprostol 800 micrograms with intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g over 10 minutes for tone augmentation
options.rationale: Misoprostol is reserved for settings without injectable uterotonics or as adjunct after first and second line agents have failed. Calcium gluconate is not a uterotonic. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Intravenous oxytocin further bolus of 10 units repeated every five minutes until uterine tone is restored
options.rationale: Bolus oxytocin in this dose can cause hypotension and is not the recommended escalation; the algorithm moves to ergometrine after the oxytocin infusion is established. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Intramuscular carboprost 250 micrograms with concurrent insertion of an intrauterine Bakri balloon for tamponade
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is an option after pharmacological measures fail, not before ergometrine has been trialled. Carboprost is similarly third line. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Primary postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is blood loss of 500 mL or more in the first 24 hours after birth, or any blood loss causing haemodynamic compromise. The 'four Ts' (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) frame the workup. The Australian uterotonic ladder is oxytocin infusion, then ergometrine 250 micrograms IM (or carbetocin 100 micrograms IV in caesarean), then carboprost 250 micrograms IM up to eight doses, then misoprostol 800 micrograms PR or SL. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given concurrently if within three hours of birth. Mechanical (bimanual compression, balloon tamponade) and surgical (B-Lynch, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy) measures follow.
key_points: Oxytocin infusion 40 units in 1 L over 4 hours after the initial 10 units IM
Ergometrine 250 micrograms IM (contraindicated in hypertension, pre-eclampsia)
Carboprost 250 micrograms IM, repeat up to 8 doses (contraindicated in asthma)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial)
Mechanical: bimanual compression, intrauterine balloon tamponade; surgical: B-Lynch, internal iliac ligation, hysterectomy
explanation.pearls: WOMAN trial: tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth reduces death due to bleeding by 31 percent (number needed to treat about 250); after 3 hours there is no mortality benefit and possible harm from clot stabilisation.

stem:
A 36-year-old G2P1 woman of South Asian background is reviewed at the joint obstetric-endocrine clinic at 30 weeks gestation. She was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks on a positive 75 g OGTT. She has completed two weeks of structured dietitian-led carbohydrate counselling and 30 minutes of post-meal walking. She is monitoring fasting and one hour post-meal capillary blood glucose four times per day.

Her body mass index pre-pregnancy was 27.6 kg/m^2. Foetal growth ultrasound at 28 weeks showed abdominal circumference on the 78th centile and estimated foetal weight on the 72nd centile. There has been no maternal hypoglycaemia. She has no proteinuria, normal blood pressure, and no obstetric or medical contraindications to oral therapy or insulin. The seven-day capillary record is summarised below.

data_table.Investigations: fasting glucose 24 of 28 readings above 5.0 mmol/L (range 4.9 to 6.4), one hour post-meal glucose 18 of 63 readings above 7.4 mmol/L (range 5.6 to 8.8), HbA1c at diagnosis 5.4 percent, no ketonuria, urine albumin:creatinine ratio normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue lifestyle and dietary measures alone with reassessment of capillary blood glucose readings in two weeks
options.rationale: ADIPS thresholds are exceeded persistently (fasting target less than 5.0, one hour post-meal less than 7.4); ongoing reliance on diet and exercise risks foetal macrosomia, polyhydramnios, and neonatal hypoglycaemia. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

options.text: Commence rapid acting insulin aspart 4 units subcutaneously before each main meal only without basal coverage
options.rationale: Prandial insulin alone targets post-meal glucose; here the dominant abnormality is fasting hyperglycaemia, which needs basal insulin. Prandial may be added later if post-meal targets are not met. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily with a stop date if foetal growth accelerates beyond the 95th centile on scan
options.rationale: Metformin is acceptable second line or where insulin is declined; ADIPS and RANZCOG prefer insulin because of better fasting glycaemic control and the long term neonatal safety data. Metformin crosses the placenta and ongoing offspring data are accumulating. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

options.text: Commence basal insulin (isophane or detemir) 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg subcutaneously at bedtime and titrate to fasting target
options.rationale: Fasting hyperglycaemia (most readings above 5.0 over two weeks of lifestyle optimisation) is best treated with basal insulin starting at 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg at bedtime and titrating to a fasting target of less than 5.0 mmol/L. ADIPS supports insulin as first line pharmacotherapy in GDM. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

options.text: Commence glibenclamide 2.5 mg orally daily and continue dietetic review monthly for the remainder of pregnancy
options.rationale: Sulfonylureas are not Australian first line for GDM; glibenclamide crosses the placenta and increases neonatal hypoglycaemia compared with insulin. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian Diabetes in Pregnancy Society (ADIPS) thresholds for self-monitoring are fasting less than 5.0 mmol/L and one or two hour post-meal less than 7.4 or 6.7 mmol/L respectively. After two weeks of dietary and exercise optimisation, persistent above-target readings warrant pharmacotherapy. Insulin remains first line: basal (NPH or detemir) at bedtime for fasting hyperglycaemia, rapid acting (aspart) pre-meal for post-meal hyperglycaemia, doses titrated every three days. Metformin is acceptable second line. Targets reduce macrosomia, polyhydramnios, neonatal hypoglycaemia, and shoulder dystocia.
key_points: ADIPS targets: fasting < 5.0; 1 hour post-meal < 7.4; 2 hour post-meal < 6.7 mmol/L
Insulin is first line; basal for fasting hyperglycaemia, prandial for post-meal
Starting dose basal insulin 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg at bedtime
Metformin acceptable second line if insulin declined
All GDM women should have 75 g OGTT 6 to 12 weeks postpartum
explanation.pearls: Pre-pregnancy BMI above 35 plus persistent fasting glucose above 5.0 on diet doubles the chance of needing insulin during pregnancy; start basal insulin without waiting for the four-week food diary trial.

stem:
A 34-year-old woman attends general practice asking to restart the combined oral contraceptive pill after a four year gap. She is otherwise well, a non-smoker, with a body mass index of 24.6 kg/m^2 and no personal history of venous thromboembolism. Her mother had a deep vein thrombosis at age 58 following an orthopaedic operation but no younger relatives are affected.

She has migraine with aura twice a year (visual scintillations followed by unilateral throbbing headache) which respond to oral sumatriptan. Her last episode was six weeks ago. Blood pressure is 122/78. She has had three normal cervical screening test results and no abnormal bleeding. She does not want a long-acting reversible contraceptive method and has not had children.

data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 134 g/L, thyroid function within normal limits, urine pregnancy test negative, blood pressure repeated 122/78

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence a 30 microgram levonorgestrel combined oral contraceptive pill from day one of her next menstrual period
options.rationale: Migraine with aura at any age is a UKMEC and WHO MEC category 4 (unacceptable risk) for combined hormonal contraception because of the doubled ischaemic stroke risk; absolute risk is small but synergistic with oestrogen. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Contraception)

options.text: Refer for full thrombophilia screening before deciding on a combined hormonal method or a progestogen-only method
options.rationale: Routine thrombophilia screening is not recommended before contraceptive prescribing because of low yield and management does not change in the absence of a personal VTE history. Migraine with aura already excludes the combined pill. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Contraception)

options.text: Commence the combined vaginal ring on the basis that lower systemic oestrogen exposure is safe in migraine with aura
options.rationale: All combined hormonal contraceptives (pill, patch, ring) share the migraine-with-aura contraindication regardless of the route, because systemic oestrogen exposure is the relevant exposure. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Contraception)

options.text: Continue with a combined oral contraceptive but advise her to stop immediately if she has another migraine with aura
options.rationale: Migraine with aura is a baseline contraindication to combined hormonal contraception; she should not be commenced on it with a conditional plan to stop. The risk is established before any further events. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Contraception)

options.text: Commence the progestogen-only desogestrel pill 75 micrograms daily or recommend depot medroxyprogesterone instead
options.rationale: Progestogen-only methods (desogestrel pill, depot medroxyprogesterone) and intrauterine devices (copper or levonorgestrel) are UKMEC category 1 or 2 in migraine with aura. The progestogen-only pill is a reasonable starting point given her preference against LARC. (Source: Family Planning Australia: Contraception)

explanation.summary: The UK Medical Eligibility Criteria (UKMEC, used in Australia by Family Planning) classify contraceptive options against medical conditions on a 1 (no restriction) to 4 (unacceptable risk) scale. Migraine with aura is category 4 for combined hormonal contraception (any route) at any age due to roughly doubled ischaemic stroke risk. Progestogen-only methods, copper IUD, and levonorgestrel IUD are category 1 or 2. Hypertension above 140/90 controlled, BMI 30 to 34, and age 35 or older with light smoking are commonly missed UKMEC restrictions.
key_points: Migraine with aura: UKMEC 4 for any combined hormonal contraception
Migraine without aura: UKMEC 2 for initiation, UKMEC 3 for continuation
BP 140 to 159/90 to 99: UKMEC 3 for CHC; BP > 160/100: UKMEC 4
Smoking > 15 per day plus age 35 or older: UKMEC 4 for CHC
All progestogen-only methods, copper IUD, and LNG-IUD are UKMEC 1 or 2 in migraine with aura
explanation.pearls: After 6 weeks postpartum a woman who has never had migraine with aura before pregnancy who develops aura on the combined pill should switch to a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method - the new aura raises ischaemic stroke risk to UKMEC 4 level.

sources.label: UK Medical Eligibility Criteria for Contraceptive Use
